status_notes: Source-level entries only — no article subpaths. Official translations (japaneselawtranslation.go.jp) return HTTP 403 to both plain HTTP fetches and a real browser (Playwright), even with browser-realistic headers; appears to be a network/WAF-level block rather than a JS-rendering issue. Structure below is sourced from secondary legal-summary sources (ICLG, Chambers Global Practice Guides), not the primary text. Revisit if the official translation becomes reachable.
notes: See also registry/entity/jp/go/cyber.json (the NCO/NISC organization itself) and registry/control/jp/go/cyber.json (its control-type publications: Common Standards for Government Entities, Cybersecurity Policy for Critical Infrastructure Protection) for the other type-specific files under this same namespace. This regulation file covers the two laws administered through the NCO/Cybersecurity Strategic Headquarters: the Basic Act on Cybersecurity and the Active Cyber Defense Act.

match_nodes.description: Basic Act on Cybersecurity — Japan's foundational cybersecurity law

data.notes: Act No. 104 of 2014, effective January 2015. Establishes the Cybersecurity Strategic Headquarters, mandates a national Cybersecurity Strategy, and authorizes the Common Standards for Cybersecurity Measures of Governmental Entities (Article 26(1)). Full-text article-level structure not yet extracted (see status_notes on the parent namespace).

urls.note: Returns HTTP 403 to automated and browser-based fetches as of 2026-07-16.

match_nodes.description: Active Cyber Defense Act — Japan's first proactive/active cyber-defense law

data.official_name: Act on Prevention of Damage Caused by Unauthorized Acts Against Important Computers
data.notes: Act No. 42 of Reiwa 7 (2025). Promulgated/enacted May 2025; general provisions took effect July 1, 2025, with core incident-reporting obligations expected around October 1, 2026 and full operational deployment targeted FY2027. A companion 'arrangement act' (Act No. 43 of 2025) amends related laws, including interplay with the Telecommunications Business Act's secrecy-of-communications guarantee (secid:regulation/soumu.go.jp/telecommunications-business-act). Four main components: (1) government authority to acquire/analyze communications data for threat detection, (2) mandatory incident reporting by 'Special Social Infrastructure Operators' / critical-infrastructure operators, (3) public-private information-sharing agreements, (4) government authority to counter-access and neutralize attacker infrastructure, under new oversight by a Cyber Communications Information Management Committee (established April 2026). Japan's first 'active defense' law — structurally comparable to the EU's NIS2 Directive in its reporting-obligation scope, but distinct in authorizing government network monitoring and counter-access.

urls.note: No official English statutory translation located as of 2026-07-16 (too recently enacted); japaneselawtranslation.go.jp does not yet list it.
Law-firm practice guide summary — fetched successfully, no block.
